title: Confusion over UK sanctions on Russia undermines a Western response that was already too slow and half-hearted
gist: Nearly four and a half years into the war, the West's 20-plus sanctions packages on Russia have failed to deliver a knockout blow because the West has prioritized minimizing its own costs over maximizing Russia's, and the UK's botched delay of diesel/jet-fuel sanctions this week exposed both a coordination failure with Europe and a weakening of post-Brexit UK-EU-NATO cohesion.

themes.theme: Structural failure of the Western sanctions campaign

insights.claim: Nearly four and a half years after Russia's full-scale invasion of Ukraine, the West still has not found a sanctions package (the EU alone has issued twenty) capable of decisively turning the course of the war.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Cumulative sanctions volume is not a proxy for sanctions effectiveness; investors should not assume further packages will be decisive either.
insights.locator: Nearly four and a half years after Russia's full-scale invasion

insights.claim: Sanctions inherently hurt both the target and the initiator, so the real skill is ensuring the target is hurt more; the West, in trying to limit the pain on itself, has pulled its punches in hurting Russia.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A generalizable framework for evaluating any sanctions regime: measure relative pain distribution, not headline package counts.
insights.quote: in trying to limit the pain on itself, the West has pulled its punches in hurting Russia
insights.locator: But in trying to limit the pain on itself

insights.claim: The West's push for a unified front through fair burden-sharing produced sanctions set at the lowest common denominator, which has been quite low, because no single country has been willing to bear much of the cost.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Unity-focused sanctions design structurally caps how aggressive Western sanctions can be, an argument for why sanctions escalation will likely remain gradual.
insights.locator: Slowing the response has been the desire to present Putin

insights.claim: The West has failed to properly treat Russia as an existential threat, which is why governments have not been honest with their electorates that countering Russia requires accepting real costs such as shortages and higher prices on products where Russia is key to supply chains.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Sanctions escalation is politically capped by governments' unwillingness to prepare voters for cost-of-living impacts, a durable constraint on future policy.
insights.locator: I would argue here that the West has continuously failed

insights.claim: Vladimir Putin likely planned for this war and built sanctions-resilience buffers as far back as the first Western sanctions following the 2014 annexation of Crimea, deleveraging the economy and building foreign exchange reserves to record levels.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Long-horizon sanctions-proofing by Russia means Western sanctions face a much higher bar to be economically decisive than initially assumed in 2022.
insights.locator: Part of the problem is that Russian President Vladimir Putin

insights.claim: Russia used its leverage over European energy supplies to manipulate energy markets and earn windfall profits both before and after the invasion, further bolstering its sanctions-resilience buffers.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Russia's own windfall energy profits partly financed its resistance to the sanctions meant to constrain it, an ironic self-defeating dynamic in early Western energy policy.
insights.locator: Russia deleveraged to reduce its vulnerabilities

themes.theme: UK's diesel and jet fuel sanctions reversal

insights.claim: The UK had signalled in October it would tighten sanctions by banning purchases of diesel and jet fuel refined in third countries from Russian crude, but after the Iran war pushed up global energy prices and the US eased sanctions on Russian oil majors Lukoil and Rosneft, the UK this week issued general licences delaying the imposition of its own diesel and jet-fuel sanctions.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A concrete example of energy-security fears (Iran war spillover) directly reversing announced Russia sanctions escalation.
insights.locator: The UK was eager to build upon US sanctions on Lukoil and Rosneft

insights.claim: This was a delay or watering-down of an already-agreed tightening, not a lifting of existing sanctions, but it created the damaging perception that the UK had gone soft on Russia and put its own consumer interests ahead of Ukraine's defenders.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Sanctions communication/optics can undermine alliance cohesion even when the substantive policy change is more modest than perceived.
insights.quote: This gave the perception that the UK had gone soft on Russia
insights.locator: This gave the perception that the UK had gone soft on Russia

insights.claim: The UK tried to counter the perception of weakness by announcing new sanctions on shipping Russian LNG and a raft of new individual and corporate designations, but new designations barely move the dial against thousands already sanctioned, and the UK itself hardly buys Russian LNG, while Spain, Belgium and France are Europe's biggest Russian LNG buyers.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: The UK's compensating LNG shipping sanctions cost it little while asking allies to bear the real burden, undermining the credibility of the move.
insights.locator: And the problem with the restrictions on shipping of LNG

insights.claim: There was apparently no coordination with Europe and Ukraine before the UK eased its own diesel/jet-fuel sanctions, and the UK did not first request allied assistance for its fuel shortage, feeding a 'do as I say, not as I do' dynamic that is unlikely to please allies being asked to restrict their own Russian LNG purchases.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Lack of prior allied consultation is a specific, checkable process failure that damaged UK credibility as a sanctions leader.
insights.locator: Do as I say, not as I do

insights.claim: The episode may reflect a broader weakening of the NATO alliance and, post-Brexit, the UK's reduced ability to draw on EU support when facing supply shortfalls.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A speculative but notable read that Brexit-related institutional distance is now visibly hampering UK crisis coordination with the EU.
insights.quote: Perhaps this all shows the weakening of the NATO alliance and, post-Brexit, the UK’s inability to draw on EU support
insights.locator: Perhaps this all shows the weakening of the NATO alliance
